segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera against a dark, textured background. The scene transitions into abstract, symmetrical, kaleidoscopic visuals resembling ink blots or cellular structures, with dark, organic patterns expanding and contracting. These patterns evolve into mirrored, fractal-like designs with repeating motifs, some resembling bacteria or microscopic organisms. The visuals become increasingly complex, incorporating text overlays such as 'huaifu [destroyed palace]' and 'schachepeth [wasting away]', presented in elegant, serif fonts within dark, ink-like circles. The color palette shifts between sepia, black, and muted reds, creating a somber, almost decayed aesthetic. The man reappears intermittently, maintaining his composed demeanor, while the abstract visuals dominate the majority of the segment, suggesting a thematic connection between the speaker and the symbolic, decaying imagery. The overall atmosphere is introspective and ominous, blending human presence with abstract representations of decay and consumption.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who speaks with a serious expression, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair clearly visible. The visuals then shift to a title card reading 'THE DEADLIEST INFECTIOUS DISEASE OF ALL TIME' in bold red serif font, followed by a 'CC' logo within a red apple shape and the text 'A CRASH COURSE LECTURE'. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, now standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting with a chalkboard behind him, displaying dated medical notes such as 'Jan. 26, 1909' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions'. The scene cuts to a split-screen montage of sepia-toned historical medical photographs: a doctor examining a patient in 1933, a nurse attending to a patient in bed, and a sign for 'HOSPITAL DEPENDARE'. A text overlay states '90% of people die of disease', then changes to '90% of natural causes', highlighting the lethality of infectious diseases. The Man in Blue Suit returns, continuing his lecture with animated hand gestures, maintaining the educational and somber tone.

active_entities.Historical Artwork Montage: A sequence of diverse historical artworks including a medieval manuscript illustration of a man in a red hat examining a patient, surrounded by others, contributing to a theme of historical illness and mortality.
active_entities.Sepia-Toned Illustration Grid: A vintage-style grid of sepia-toned illustrations featuring a tiger, a bird, and a historical portrait, accompanied by evolving text that reads 'Tiger got to hunt, bird got to fly; Man got to sit and wonder why, why, why?' and later 'Man got to tell himself he understand,' with the final phrase underlined in red.
active_entities.Text Overlays: Elegant serif text appearing within dark, circular ink-like frames, displaying the word 'invalids' in a golden hue, contributing to a theme of historical medical terminology and societal perception of illness.
active_entities.Philosophical Text Overlay: Bold red serif text on a dark, textured background reading 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND', appearing after the Man in Blue Suit's lecture, suggesting a reflective or educational conclusion.
active_entities.Historical Medical Photographs: A split-screen montage of sepia-toned, vintage photographs showing medical scenes: a doctor examining a patient in 1933, a nurse attending to a patient in bed, and a sign for 'HOSPITAL DEPENDARE'. These images are overlaid with text stating '90% of people die of disease' and '90% of natural causes', emphasizing the historical impact of infectious diseases.
active_entities.Split-Screen Bacteria Animation: A side-by-side animated comparison showing the growth of 'Mycobacterium tuberculosis' (slow, sparse multiplication) and 'E. coli' (rapid, dense multiplication) on a brown, textured background, with labels in white serif font.
active_entities.Family Photographs: Two sepia-toned photographs: one of a man in a plaid shirt with a young boy in a scout uniform, and another of two young boys in swimwear jumping joyfully in a wooded area, evoking a nostalgic, personal history.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen: on the left, a sepia-toned grid displays three illustrations—a tiger, a bird, and a historical portrait—while on the right, the Man in Blue Suit speaks directly to the camera in a vintage classroom setting, gesturing expressively. The scene transitions to a full view of the Man in Blue Suit standing before a chalkboard with medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs,' as he continues his lecture. The video then cuts to a series of historical illustrations: a young girl in bed with a spectral figure above her, a sketch of two men at a table with wine glasses, and a portrait of Gideon Harvey with a skeleton and alchemist in the background. The Harvey portrait is shown alongside a vintage advertisement for 'Swift's Specific,' promoting it as a cure for blood poisons, with testimonials from pastors and merchants. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, speaking with intensity, before the video shifts to a sepia-toned photograph of a doctor examining a patient in bed, followed by a black-and-white image of a hospital ward with multiple patients. The final sequence shows a woman reading beside a sleeping child in a garden, then a split-screen with a portrait of Jay Gould (1836–1892) flanked by abstract, symmetrical patterns resembling spinal vertebrae and a textured geometric design, suggesting a thematic link between historical figures, illness, and biological structure.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen of sepia-toned historical portraits: James Monroe and Andrew Jackson, followed by a portrait of Eliza Poe, establishing a theme of historical figures. The scene transitions to a surreal, vintage-style black-and-white image of a woman in a dress emerging from a large clamshell on a rocky shore, with the shell opening to reveal her lying inside. This is accompanied by a portrait of Henry David Thoreau and a quote about death and decay being beautiful, like the pearly tear of shellfish or the glow of consumption. The scene then shifts to a modern-day lecture setting, where the Man in Blue Suit stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, gesturing as he speaks. The chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with listed conditions such as anemia, hemorrhage, brain tumor, and syphilis. The lighting is dim and academic, with vintage medical equipment visible on the desk. The video concludes with a brief, ghostly, monochrome image of the Man in Blue Suit’s face superimposed over abstract, swirling patterns, suggesting a transition or thematic echo.

segments.caption: The video opens with a grainy, black-and-white archival clip of a person in a lab coat, possibly a scientist, handling equipment in a dimly lit laboratory, suggesting early 20th-century medical research. This transitions to a close-up of a child’s face, eyes wide and expressive, evoking vulnerability or illness. The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, lecturing with expressive hand gestures. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with listed conditions like 'Anemia,' 'Hemorrhage,' 'Brain Tumor,' and 'Syphilis.' The names 'Professor Sternk' and 'Dr. Jackson' are also visible. The video then cuts to a split-screen montage of vintage medical footage: a nurse attending to a patient in bed, a woman lying in bed with a nurse beside her, and a wide shot of a hospital ward with multiple beds and staff. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture with animated gestures. A title card then appears with the text 'THE ALLURE OF CONSUMPTION' in bold, golden serif font against a dark, textured background. The video returns to the lecturer, who continues speaking, before transitioning to a final title card with the words 'human soul:' in elegant white script over a swirling, abstract, sepia-toned background resembling organic matter or cellular structures, suggesting a thematic shift toward the philosophical or spiritual aspects of illness and consumption.

segments.caption: The video opens with an abstract, kaleidoscopic background of swirling, organic textures in sepia and dark brown tones, overlaid with the elegant white text 'human soul:'. Below, four square frames appear sequentially, each displaying a word for 'soul' in different languages: 'ruach' [Hebrew], 'chi' [Chinese], 'spirit' [English], and 'sila' [Inuit], each within a circular, ink-blot-like design. The background then shifts to a faint X-ray image of a human chest, with the word 'breath' appearing in quotation marks, followed by 'inspired' [to breathe in] and 'expired' [to breathe all the way out], emphasizing the linguistic and biological connection between breath and life. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him listing medical topics from 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions'. He gestures expressively as he speaks. The video then cuts to a series of historical artworks: a Pre-Raphaelite-style painting of a woman with closed eyes, seemingly in a trance or prayer, with a glowing aura and a bird in the background; a sepia-toned photograph of a man in formal 19th-century attire seated at a desk in a study, deep in thought; and a triptych of three sepia-toned portraits of men with distinct facial hair and formal wear, suggesting historical figures. The Man in Blue Suit reappears in a close-up, continuing his lecture with a serious expression, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair clearly visible, as he speaks directly to the camera.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who speaks directly to the camera with a serious and engaged expression, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair clearly visible. The scene transitions to a wider shot, revealing him standing behind a wooden desk in the Vintage Classroom Setting, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' with subpoints like 'Anemia,' 'Hemorrhage,' 'Brain Tumor,' and 'Syphilis.' He gestures expressively as he lectures, emphasizing his points. The video then cuts to a split-screen montage: on the left, a sequence of Historical Artworks Montage featuring a Pre-Raphaelite-style painting of a contemplative man, a sepia-toned photograph of a man in distress at a desk, and another of a man writing at a desk; on the right, a beige card displays a quote about 'waywardness, peevishness, irascibility, misanthropy, [and] murky passions' being 'referable to their constitutional peculiarities and condition,' concluding with 'In simple words that their mental eccentricities result from the derangement of bodily health.' The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated hand gestures, reinforcing the historical and medical themes. The lighting remains consistent, dim and academic, enhancing the vintage atmosphere of the classroom.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who speaks directly to the camera with a serious expression, his blue-rimmed glasses and graying hair visible as he delivers a lecture. The background is a dark chalkboard with faint medical text, setting a scholarly tone. The scene transitions to a split-screen: on the left, a beige card displays a quote from Lord Byron, 'I should like, I think, to die of consumption... because then the women would all say, ‘See that poor Byron – how interesting he looks in dying!’' On the right, a sepia-toned portrait of Lord Byron appears, showing him in profile with his hand on his chin, dressed in period attire against a floral-patterned backdrop. The video then cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit, now standing behind a wooden desk in the Vintage Classroom Setting, gesturing expressively as he continues his lecture. The chalkboard behind him is clearly visible, displaying notes such as 'Jan. 26, 1909 2:00 pm', 'Pathology of Gross Lesions', and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. The scene shifts again to a triptych of historical artworks: a woman in a black dress standing beside a table, a woman in a white lace gown reclining in a wicker chair, and a sepia-toned portrait of a woman in a dark dress with lace collar, holding a book. The left panel then displays a quote from Charlotte Brontë: 'Consumption, I am aware, is a flattering malady.' The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit for a brief moment before cutting to a series of historical artworks: a painting of a young girl in a red dress standing in a dimly lit room with a bedridden figure, followed by a watercolor of a woman in a green dress sitting by a window, gazing out at a pastoral landscape. The final image is a close-up of the same woman, her face serene, with the landscape softly blurred in the background, evoking a sense of quiet contemplation and illness.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a watercolor painting of a young girl in a red dress standing beside a bedridden figure in a dimly lit room, evoking themes of illness and care. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who stands in a vintage classroom setting, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' The video then cuts to a sepia-toned portrait of Eliza Poe, a woman with dark, curly hair adorned with a ribbon, wearing a light-colored dress with a low neckline, set against a dark background. This is followed by a vintage engraved portrait of Edgar Allan Poe, framed ornately with the name 'EDGAR ALLAN POE' beneath. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, gesturing emphatically as the phrase 'consumptive chic' appears in elegant serif text on a red banner. The video then presents a split-screen montage of vintage advertisements: one for A.L. Moody’s Corset Dept. and another for a dandruff remedy with the slogan 'To be Pretty, A Woman’s Hair Must be free From Dandruff.' This is followed by another split-screen showing a 'BRIGHT-EYES GOOD-BYE' advertisement and a painting of a young girl in a blue dress holding a basket of cherries. The sequence continues with a close-up of a woman’s eyes, then transitions to a vintage trade card for 'ENAMEL AMERICA' by François Grégoire & Co., featuring a woman holding an American flag, with the text 'FOR BEAUTIFYING AND PRESERVING THE COMPLEXION.' The final shot is a close-up of the woman’s face from the trade card, emphasizing her pale complexion and large eyes, reinforcing the theme of 'consumptive chic' and the historical idealization of illness in beauty standards.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen montage of fashion imagery: on the left, a model in a dark, structured blue dress with dramatic ruffles and a hand on her hip, and on the right, a model in a black lace dress with a sheer, ruffled neckline, adjusting a bracelet. This transitions to a triptych of vintage advertisements: a colorful 1950s-style ad for Pepsi-Cola featuring a woman in a white dress with the slogan 'Fashion is for the Slender,' a black-and-white Calvin Klein 'OBSESSION' ad with a model in a black top against a textured wall, and a purple magazine cover with a woman in a yellow top and the text 'HEALTHY WEEKLY FOR WOMEN.' The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, gesturing as he speaks, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' The video then shows a split-screen of historical illustrations: on the left, a colorized Victorian woman in a red dress and blue gloves holding flowers, and on the right, a black-and-white engraving of a woman in a dark dress with a skeleton beside her, holding flowers. This is followed by a triptych of corset advertisements: two black-and-white images of women in tightly laced corsets flanking a central vintage trade card for 'THOMSON'S PATENT GLOVE-FITTING CORSETS,' showing a woman adjusting her corset in front of a mirror. The Man in Blue Suit reappears in a small inset at the top, while below, two text overlays appear on a dark, patterned background: 'waste' and 'waist,' highlighting the linguistic evolution of the word. The video then shows a black-and-white close-up of a corset being laced tightly, followed by a vintage photograph of a woman lying in bed, seemingly ill, with a nurse attending to her. The Man in Blue Suit returns, continuing his lecture. The final scene is a split-screen: on the left, a sepia-toned portrait of a bearded man in profile, and on the right, a beige card with a quote attributed to Charles Dickens: 'There is a dread disease, in which the struggle between soul and body is so gradual, quiet, and solemn... that the spirit grows light and sanguine with its lightening load.'

segments.caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned portrait of a bearded man in profile, accompanied by a quote attributed to Charles Dickens on a beige card, describing a 'dread disease' where the soul gradually separates from the body, leaving the spirit 'light and sanguine.' The scene transitions to a vintage classroom setting where the Man in Blue Suit stands behind a wooden desk, lecturing in front of a chalkboard filled with medical notes from January 26, 1909, including topics like 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He gestures expressively as he speaks, his blue-rimmed glasses and serious demeanor emphasizing the academic tone. The video then cuts to a classical portrait of John Keats, labeled with his name, followed by black-and-white archival footage of the 'Krankenhaus der Stadt Wien' (Vienna City Hospital) and a group of medical professionals in white coats. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture with focused intensity, his gestures and facial expressions conveying the gravity of the subject matter. The sequence ends with a close-up of his face, underscoring the emotional weight of the narrative.

segments.caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned, textured background featuring symmetrical, ink-blot-like patterns that resemble fractal or cellular decay, setting a somber, historical tone. The title 'THE WHITE MAN'S PLAGUE' appears in bold, red serif font against a dark, grungy backdrop, establishing the theme. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, speaking directly to the camera with a serious expression. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs,' reinforcing the historical and academic context. The video then cuts to a split-screen montage of black-and-white archival footage: on the left, a classroom scene with students and a teacher, and on the right, a nurse interacting with patients in a hospital setting. This is followed by a lab scene showing a scientist working with test tubes, juxtaposed with a close-up of a handwritten hospital record. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, now with a text overlay quoting 'a disease of the master race not of the slave race,' emphasizing the racial undertones of the historical medical narrative. The sequence concludes with the Man in Blue Suit continuing his lecture, framed by the vintage classroom environment, as the text overlay remains on screen, underscoring the video’s critical examination of historical medical racism.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dark, abstract background featuring symmetrical, ink-blot-like patterns that evolve into fractal, mirrored designs resembling bacteria or cellular decay. Over this, a text overlay in elegant serif font appears, quoting Frank Snowden: “In the United States, the prevailing wisdom was that African Americans contracted a different disease...” The background shifts to a black-and-white historical map of the world, with lines radiating from various locations, suggesting global connections or disease spread. The scene transitions to a close-up of a filing cabinet with labeled drawers for places like 'CAYMAN ISLANDS,' 'ST. HELENA,' and 'KENYA,' as a hand pulls out a file. This is followed by archival footage of people in colonial-era attire, including men in hats and women in long dresses, gathered in outdoor settings, possibly at a medical or administrative outpost. Split-screen shots show a map of India and a crowd of people, then a man standing by a rocky cliffside and a map of South Africa. The footage continues with scenes of people lining up at a table, possibly for medical examination, and a large crowd in a city street, with buildings and a vintage car visible. The final scene shifts to a modern setting: a man in a blue suit and glasses stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He gestures expressively as he speaks, his expression serious and engaged, suggesting a lecture or educational presentation. The lighting is warm and focused on him, contrasting with the dim, historical footage that preceded it.

segments.caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, delivering a lecture. The chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including topics like 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with names such as 'Professor Sternk' and 'Dr. Jackson' written on the right. He gestures expressively as he speaks, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses. The scene then cuts to a series of grainy, blue-tinted archival clips: a man and woman in an intimate embrace, followed by a street scene with buildings and a crowd, then a black-and-white shot of people walking in a city, some carrying bundles. The footage shifts to a dimly lit interior, possibly a factory or workshop, with workers around large machinery, and then to a crowded room with rows of people seated at desks, possibly a classroom or office. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture. The final sequence is a triptych: two sepia-toned portraits of a bearded man with glasses and a bow tie (likely Robert Koch) flank a central panel showing a microscopic view of rod-shaped bacteria labeled 'm. tuberculosis' with the year '1882' below, and a lower panel showing a magnified view of similar bacteria. The text 'm. tuberculosis' appears in elegant script over the central image, emphasizing the historical discovery of the tuberculosis bacterium.

The sequence transitions to a vintage classroom setting where the Man in Blue Suit stands behind a wooden desk, lecturing in front of a chalkboard inscribed with medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' The overall atmosphere is somber and historically charged, blending educational discourse with disturbing racialized medical narratives.

segments.caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, delivering a lecture with expressive hand gestures. The chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including topics like 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with listed conditions such as Anemia, Hemorrhage, Brain Tumor, and Syphilis. The setting is dimly lit, evoking an early 20th-century academic atmosphere, with vintage medical bottles and equipment visible on the side. As he speaks, the camera zooms in for a close-up of his face, emphasizing his serious expression and blue-rimmed glasses. The scene then transitions to a series of black-and-white historical photographs and text overlays. First, a photograph shows a man in a white coat writing at a desk, followed by a text overlay quoting, 'the negro is to blame for his own susceptibility to tuberculosis,' attributed to W. Michael Byrd and Linda A. Clayton from 'An American Health Dilemma: Race, Medicine, and Health Care in the United States.' This is followed by a split-screen showing a classroom scene with students and a teacher on the left, and a nurse interacting with patients in a hospital setting on the right. The final frame displays a critical quote: 'smacked more of the cheap politician seeking notoriety and office by playing to passion and prejudice than a doctor discussing, philosophically, a scientific subject for the diffusion of knowledge,' also from the same book, overlaid on two archival images—one of a lab with test tubes and another of people boarding a mobile medical unit. The sequence juxtaposes the lecturer’s academic tone with historical racialized medical discourse, highlighting systemic bias in public health narratives.

segments.caption: The video opens with a series of grainy, vintage archival clips depicting medical and public health scenes: a group of nurses in white uniforms gather around a patient in a hospital setting, followed by a close-up of a doctor in a white coat examining a document, and then a bustling outdoor scene with people walking near a mobile medical unit under a bright sky. The overall narrative flows from historical medical imagery to a contemporary educational commentary, punctuated by a powerful quote that critiques the global disparity in healthcare access.

segments.caption: The video opens with a still photograph of a young woman in a red jacket and blue jeans standing against a pink wall, her expression somber. A text overlay appears, quoting Phumeza Tisile: “So I went to the clinic and coughed into a cup.” The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He speaks with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points. The video then cuts to black-and-white archival footage of a scientist in a lab, intently working with a microscope and glassware, followed by a close-up of a microscopic view of a rod-shaped bacterium. This is succeeded by a historical illustration of Robert Koch from 1882, also at a microscope, surrounded by lab equipment. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture. The final shot is an X-ray of a human chest with dense, cloudy areas in the lungs, overlaid with the text: “I was really struggling to breathe.” The sequence blends personal testimony, historical medical discovery, and educational narration to convey the gravity of tuberculosis.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen montage of hospital environments: on the left, a person in an orange jumpsuit walks through a green-walled hallway with glass doors, while on the right, a crowded waiting room shows patients and medical staff in a clinical setting. The scene transitions to a hospital room where a man in an orange jumpsuit sits on a bed labeled 'BED 2', hugging his knees, while a nurse in blue scrubs prepares supplies at a desk. The video then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. He speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands, as text overlays appear: a quote from Dr. Jennifer Furin stating, 'Tuberculosis is the only disease I know where the core of therapy is based on fundamental mistrust,' followed by the phrase 'patient noncompliance' in a stylized font. The scene briefly cuts to a close-up of a man in a white shirt walking through a dimly lit corridor before returning to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated hand movements. The segment ends with a final text overlay: 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND' in bold red serif font on a dark, textured background, suggesting a philosophical conclusion to the medical discourse.

segments.caption: The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom setting, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including topics like 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions,' with names such as 'Professor Sterne' and 'Dr. Jackson' written on the right. The setting is dimly lit, evoking an early 20th-century academic atmosphere, with vintage medical bottles and equipment visible on the side. The scene then cuts to a close-up of a young woman in a pink shirt wearing a teal surgical mask, looking through window blinds with a pensive expression. Text overlays appear, identifying her condition as 'multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB)'. The video transitions to a chest X-ray image with a quote superimposed: 'I was searching stuff online, and it was really, really scary to see because on Google so many of the people on Images were already dead. Their ribs were exposed, and I thought I was gonna be like that, too. I thought I was likely going to die.' The quote is attributed to Phumeza Tisile. The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, maintaining the same vintage classroom backdrop.

segments.caption: The video opens with a split-screen: on the left, a black-and-white historical photograph of a bald man with round glasses and a mustache, identified as Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams, and on the right, a textured dark background with a quote appearing in elegant serif font: “poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, bad working conditions, long hours, high rent, [and] poor food.” The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He speaks with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points. The video then cuts to a montage of historical artworks and photographs, including a medieval manuscript illustration, a portrait of a woman in a green dress, and a painting of a crowded hospital ward, before returning to the Man in Blue Suit. The final segment features a bar graph on a sepia-toned background, showing a decline in numbers from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032, suggesting a projection or unknown future trend.

segments.caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned bar graph showing a decline in numbers from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032, suggesting a projection or unknown future trend. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom setting, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points. The video then cuts to a modern-day scene showing a young Black man in an orange 'Partners In Health' t-shirt with a stethoscope around his neck, wearing a face mask and a cap, standing outdoors with greenery and flowers in the background. This is followed by a brief shot of a woman in a patterned blouse walking through a hallway with papers pinned to the wall. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, before zooming in for a close-up of his face as he speaks intently. The scene briefly fades to black, then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now with a split-screen effect: on the left, he continues speaking, and on the right, a dark, abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern appears with the bold red text 'TB FIGHTERS' overlaid, suggesting a thematic shift to modern tuberculosis advocacy.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who is speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points. He is wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, standing in front of a dark, textured background. On the right side of the split-screen, a red and black 'TB FIGHTERS' logo appears with the website 'tbfighters.org' below it, set against an abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern resembling ink blots or cellular decay. The scene transitions to a wider shot, revealing the Vintage Classroom Setting with a large chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' The Man in Blue Suit continues his lecture, gesturing with both hands, while vintage medical equipment and bottles are visible on the desk and shelves. The video then shifts to a full-screen credits sequence, listing production roles such as Executive Producers Hank Green and John Green, Host/Writer John Green, and others, with the text displayed in red and white serif font on a dark, textured background. The credits also acknowledge filming at the Indiana Medical History Museum and include special thanks to various individuals. The segment concludes with a final screen showing 'Archival Source Credits' from institutions like the National Library of Medicine and the Smithsonian American Art Museum, followed by a transition to a bright blue background with animated colorful lines forming the 'COMPLEXLY' logo in white text with a blue circular 'C' icon.
